coredata.dc:title: Magnetic properties of laterally connected super-zethrene chains: Insights from spin-polarized calculations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Abdelsalam, Hazem
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the optimized molecular structures, stability, electronic, optical, and transport properties of super-zethrene (SZ) chains connected by C-atoms. Through computational analysis, the research uncovers intriguing structural features, such as SZ's planarity compared to non-planar conformations in derivatives. Binding energy calculations reveal increasing stability with the increase of the connected SZ, with 3SZ-3C emerging as the most stable configuration. Spin-polarized calculations show that SZ has antiferromagnetic spin ordering that is kept unchanged after connection with an even number of C-atoms. In some chains connected with an odd number of C-atoms, the chain becomes ferromagnetic with spin-triplet multiplicity. Spin-up electronic characteristics exhibit consistent trends, except for 2SZ-3C, with energy gap reductions tied to higher n, notably in 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C. Quantum stability parameters and electrical conductivity evaluations emphasize desirable electronic attributes, suggesting potential applications. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) studies pinpoint reactive sites, crucial for understanding chemical behavior. UV–Vis absorption spectra demonstrate distinctive optical transitions influenced by n and m variations. Current/voltage profiles showcase unique electrical trends, highlighting saturation points linked to the number of connecting carbon atoms. Collectively, this study provides a comprehensive exploration of laterally connected SZ chains, revealing their structural, electronic, optical, magnetic, and electrical properties, offering prospects for diverse electronic and optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Super-zethrene
DFT
Magnetic properties
Electronic and optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum stability and electrical conductivity for spin-up electrons

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Ministry of Science and Technology, Foreign Experts Program (No. QN2021014007L, QN2022014009L), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361, 20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SZ and its based materials optimized molecular structures.
Graphical representation of HOMO/LUMO energies, molecular distribution, and energy gaps (Eg) for spin up electrons of SZ and its based compounds.
Spin-up DOS for SZ and its based compounds.
(a–e) Electrostatic potential map (ESP) for SZ and its based compounds.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of SZ and its based compounds.
I/V curve for SZ and its based compounds.
Some important quantum parameters like bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees) for SZ and its derivatives.
The energy gap (Eg), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), chemical hardness (η), dipole moment (μ), and electrical conductivity for spin-up of selected SZ MS and its based materials.

The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for SZ and its derivative materials.

head.ce:title: Magnetic properties of laterally connected super-zethrene chains: Insights from spin-polarized calculations

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the optimized molecular structures, stability, electronic, optical, and transport properties of super-zethrene (SZ) chains connected by C-atoms. Through computational analysis, the research uncovers intriguing structural features, such as SZ's planarity compared to non-planar conformations in derivatives. Binding energy calculations reveal increasing stability with the increase of the connected SZ, with 3SZ-3C emerging as the most stable configuration. Spin-polarized calculations show that SZ has antiferromagnetic spin ordering that is kept unchanged after connection with an even number of C-atoms. In some chains connected with an odd number of C-atoms, the chain becomes ferromagnetic with spin-triplet multiplicity. Spin-up electronic characteristics exhibit consistent trends, except for 2SZ-3C, with energy gap reductions tied to higher n, notably in 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C. Quantum stability parameters and electrical conductivity evaluations emphasize desirable electronic attributes, suggesting potential applications. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) studies pinpoint reactive sites, crucial for understanding chemical behavior. UV–Vis absorption spectra demonstrate distinctive optical transitions influenced by n and m variations. Current/voltage profiles showcase unique electrical trends, highlighting saturation points linked to the number of connecting carbon atoms. Collectively, this study provides a comprehensive exploration of laterally connected SZ chains, revealing their structural, electronic, optical, magnetic, and electrical properties, offering prospects for diverse electronic and optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, on-surface synthesis has emerged as a powerful method for the fabrication of various polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) featuring zigzag edges, including acenes [1–3], periacenes[4], anthenes[5], periacenoacenes[6], triangulenes [7], and Clar's goblet [8], many of which exhibit an open-shell ground state. Among these, zethrenes stand out as a distinct class of open-shell PAHs, characterized by their Z-shaped structure with mixed zigzag and armchair edges. The parent zethrene, first reported by Clar et al. [9], is a closed-shell compound. However, the longitudinal extension of zethrenes leads to the formation of proaromatic quinodimethane moieties, promoting an open-shell ground state. This has been demonstrated through the synthesis of derivatives from heptazethrene [10] to nonazethrene [11]. Theoretical studies have shown that, compared to closely related acenes, zethrenes exhibit a more rapid increase in radical character and a quicker decrease in the singlet–triplet gap with an increasing number of benzenoid rings [11]. Moreover, the lateral extension of zethrenes results in the formation of super-zethrene compounds, which are inherently open-shell [12]. Although the parent super-zethrene has not been realized, solution syntheses of larger homologues, such as derivatives of super-heptazethrene [13] and super-octazethrene [14], have been reported recently. Access to larger zethrenes presents an opportunity to investigate unconventional π-magnetism at the single-molecule scale and explore potential applications in molecular electronics and optics. For instance, zethrenes could serve as building blocks for high-mobility graphene nanoribbons and graphene nanoribbon-based spin filter devices[^28^], as well as function as organic near-infrared dyes [11].

Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) exhibit a wide range of physical and chemical properties that are both of fundamental interest and practical significance [15,16]. These properties can be adjusted by altering the molecular size, shape, and edge structure of the PAHs. For example, PAHs with edges shaped like armchairs are generally stable and possess large energy gaps between their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [15]. On the other hand, PAHs with edges shaped like zigzags are more reactive, have relatively smaller HOMO-LUMO gaps and may possess open-shell (magnetic) ground states [16]. This variability offers exciting possibilities in fields like molecular electronics, nonlinear optics, and quantum computation.

Conjugated polycyclic hydrocarbons, referred to as nanographene (NGs), have captivated researchers due to their tunable properties hinging on factors such as size, shape, and, notably, edge structure [16]. Of particular interest are NGs with zigzag edges, which may possess an open-shell ground state, rendering them attractive for applications in molecular electronics and spintronics [16–18]. However, the synthesis of open-shell NGs in solution presents an enduring challenge due to their reactivity, often necessitating thermodynamic and kinetic stabilization strategies [16,19]. Prominent instances of successfully synthesized open-shell NGs in solution encompass anthenes, periacenes, zethrenes, as well as non-Kekulé frameworks like phenalenyl radical and triangulene [19–23]. Alternatively, on-surface synthesis under ultrahigh vacuum conditions emerges as a promising avenue for generating open-shell NGs, offering the ability to characterize individual molecules using scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and spectroscopy (STS) [24]. Notably, in recent years, challenging open-shell NGs like triangulene and its p-extended homologues, Clar’s goblet, and zigzag graphene nanoribbons have been synthesized on metal and insulator surfaces, enabling the exploration of all-carbon magnetism at the nanoscale [7,8,25,26].

Among the diverse NGs, the family of zethrenes is notable; these structures exhibit a Z-shaped arrangement featuring a mix of zigzag and armchair edges [27]. While the fundamental zethrene compound, incorporating a 1,3-butadiene motif, is closed-shell in nature [27], extending zethrenes longitudinally introduces proaromatic quinodimethane moieties, thereby conferring moderate diradical character to these molecules [10,11]. Remarkably, the degree of open-shell character in zethrenes is substantially enhanced through lateral extension, giving rise to the intriguing category of super-zethrene compounds [13,14]. Over the years, advancements in zethrene chemistry in solution have demonstrated their potential as candidates for near-infrared dyes, nonlinear optical chromophores, and charge transport materials [12]. In this study, we have interconnected two and three units of super zethrene (SZ) by utilizing two or three carbon atoms, resulting in the creation of four distinct SZ-derived materials: 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C. Through this approach, we aim to investigate the impact of augmenting the SZ molecular framework as well as varying the number of carbon bridges linking SZ units on the electronic/magnetic attributes, electrostatic potential distribution, and optical properties of these SZ-based materials.

Gaussian 16 [18,28–31] is used to do simulations with Density Functional Theory (DFT) [32,33,42–50,34–41]. To accurately represent the electronic and optical properties of carbon-based structures, the hybrid B3LYP functional [51,52] was employed in this study. It has been well-analyzed and demonstrated to provide reliable results for such materials [53–56]. The 6-31G basis set was chosen for the calculations, as it strikes a balance between computational efficiency and accuracy [57]. It has been widely used in similar studies due to its acceptable level of accuracy [58–61]. For analyzing the optical properties, time dependent DFT calculations were performed for the first twenty excited states. These calculations allow a detailed analysis of the electronic transitions responsible for the observed optical behavior. The current/volt curves were generated using density functional theory (DFT) and the local density approximation (LDA) method with the PWC (Perdew-Wang91-Ceperley-Alder) functional in Acceryls' code Dmol [62–66]. This approach provides valuable insights into the electrical behavior and conductivity of the studied materials.

The selection of the Super-Zethrene chain as the model system is justified by its distinctive structural and electronic features, as discussed in the literature [12,67]. These attributes contribute to its suitability for the in-depth exploration of magnetic properties. The optimized molecular structures (MSs) of super-zethrene (SZ) and its derivatives, labeled as nSZ-mC, were analyzed in this study. These derivatives have varying numbers of SZ units (n) and linked carbon atoms between SZ units (m). The results were presented in Fig. 1 (a-e) and summarized in Table 1 . From the analysis, it was observed that SZ and its based compounds exhibited interesting structural characteristics. The most notable finding was that SZ itself (Fig. 1a) is fully planar, while all other investigated structures showed non-planar conformations. The dihedral angles C5-C8-C9-C10 and C2-C3-C4-C5, which are important indicators of planarity, fell within a narrow range between 177.701° and 179.993° for SZ unit and all SZ-based materials, including 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C. Among the derivatives, 2SZ-2C and 3SZ-2C (Fig. 1 (b-c)) demonstrated relatively smaller rotations of each SZ unit compared to 2SZ-3C and 3SZ-3C (Fig. 1 (d-e)). This observation was attributed to the higher number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units in the latter two compounds. The bond angles C1-C2-C3, C4-C5-C8, and C8-C9-C10 in all studied structures were approximately 120.00°, indicating sp2 hybridization of hexagonal groups within these molecular systems. Furthermore, the length of the C1-C2 bond showed an interesting trend. It decreased as the number of SZ units and the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units increased. The C1-C2 bond lengths for SZ, 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C were measured as 1.409 Å, 1.400 Å, 1.390 Å, 1.390 Å, and 1.383 Å, respectively. In terms of C-C bond lengths, variations were observed among the studied MSs due to the presence of single and double bond properties. In summary, the investigation of SZ and its derivatives provided valuable insights into their structural characteristics, particularly their planarity, dihedral angles, and bond lengths. These findings contribute to a better understanding of the electronic and chemical properties of these fascinating molecular systems.

The investigation into the stability of SZ and its derivatives involved the calculation of their binding energy (BE). The binding energy was determined using the equation: BE = (NCEC + NHEH - Et)/Nt, where NC, NH, and Nt represent the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, and the total number of atoms, respectively. EC, EH, and Et correspond to the total energies of the carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, and the final compound, respectively. In our analysis of the calculated binding energies, we sought to confirm the stability and characteristics of SZ and its derivatives. The positive binding energies obtained, ranging from 10.682 to 11.003 eV, indicate the formation of SZ and its associated compounds in a stable manner. Upon evaluating the binding energies for SZ, 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C, we obtained the values 10.682, 10.901, 10.921, 10.974, and 11.003 eV, respectively. These values demonstrate that as the number of SZ units and the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units increases, the stability of SZ-based molecular structures (MSs) also increases. Based on the calculated values of BE, it can be concluded that the most stable molecular structure among the studied MSs is 3SZ-3C when compared to the others. In summary, the investigation into the binding energies has confirmed the stable formation of SZ and its derivatives, and the results show that the configuration 3SZ-3C exhibits the highest stability among the examined molecular structures. In contrast to previous studies on biphenyl boroxine covalent organic framework (COF) and its derivatives, which reported binding energy (BE) values in the range of 6–10 eV [30], our current manuscript demonstrates that SZ and its based materials exhibit even higher stability. The calculated BE values for SZ and its derivatives, ranging from 10.682 to 11.003 eV, clearly indicate their superior stability compared to biphenyl boroxine COF. This suggests that the SZ-based materials present a more stable molecular structure when compared to the previously studied biphenyl boroxine COF and its derivatives [30]. These findings highlight the potential advantages of SZ and its derivatives as promising candidates for stable and robust materials in various applications.

To accurately characterize the electronic properties of the examined structure, an initial step involves discerning whether the structure exhibits magnetic behavior. This is achieved by conducting calculations to determine the ground state energy of various magnetic states. These states encompass nonmagnetic (closed shell), antiferromagnetic (open shell), and ferromagnetic configurations, each with different spin multiplicities. By comparing the ground state energies of these distinct magnetic configurations, we can ascertain the most energetically favorable state for the system. Upon thorough analysis, it becomes evident that the ground state energy for all structures aligns with the antiferromagnetic state. However, there is an exception observed in the case of the 2SZ-3C configuration, depicted in Fig. 1 (e). In this instance, the spin configuration assumes a ferromagnetic triplet spin state, primarily due to the presence of unpaired electrons stemming from the third carbon atom.

The spin-up molecular orbitals in the antiferromagnetic cases show almost the same energy gap and highest occupied/lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO) distributions. Therefore, we present our discussion on the electronic properties of only spin-up molecular orbitals. On the other hand, in the case of the ferromagnetic case of 2SZ-3C, we discuss both cases of spin-up and spin-down electronic structures. This investigation offers a thorough look at the typical spin-up electrical behaviors and insightful information about the overall electronic structure. For a thorough exploration of electronic properties, we also present the spin-down electronic attributes of the 2SZ-3C compound in Fig. S1 of the supplementary material. This approach ensures a holistic understanding of electronic features across these materials, underscoring the unique electronic signature of 2SZ-3C. The analysis of spin-up highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels, along with energy gaps (Eg), is crucial for comprehending the electronic characteristics of the examined systems. The HOMO energy (EHOMO) signifies the electron-donating capacity, while the LUMO energy (ELUMO) characterizes the electron-withdrawing capability [68]. The energy gap (Eg) is defined as the difference between EHOMO and ELUMO. [68] In Fig. 2 , we depict graphical representations of spin-up electron HOMO and LUMO states for both super-zethrene (SZ) and its derivatives. Across SZ and its derivatives' molecular structures, the HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals are localized within specific regions. Fig. 2 presents the calculated energy levels of spin-up HOMO and LUMO, as well as the corresponding Eg values in the gaseous state for SZ and its derivatives. The EHOMO values for all structures studied range from −4.346 to −4.396 eV, while the ELUMO values span from −2.313 to −2.931 eV. Among the configurations, SZ exhibits the most stable spin-up HOMO energy, with 3SZ-2C displaying the lowest stable spin-up HOMO energy. Comparatively, concerning the ELUMO values, 3SZ-2C demonstrates the most stable spin-up LUMO energy, while 2SZ-3C MS displays the least stability. The order of Eg values for the spin-up molecular states follows 3SZ-2C < 2SZ-2C < 3SZ-3C < SZ < 2SZ-3C. As such, increasing the number of SZ units and reducing the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units correlates with a decrease in the Eg value. This range of energy gaps spans from 1.415 eV to 2.066 eV. Notably, the lowest spin-up energy gap is observed in 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C, indicating that the number of SZ units has a more pronounced impact on the spin-up energy gap than the presence of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ-based MSs. This observation suggests that structural modifications involving the number of SZ units contribute significantly to the modulation of the spin-up energy gap in these systems. To support the data in Fig. 2, the spin-up electronic density of states (DOS) is depicted in Fig. 3 (a-e) for SZ and its derivatives. The energy levels are obtained using Gaussian functions with broadening. It is evident from Fig. 3 (a-e) that the spin-up energy gap strongly depends on the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units and the number of SZ MSs. In conclusion, the analysis of the spin-up HOMO/LUMO molecular orbitals and energy gaps reveals the electronic characteristics of SZ and its derivatives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum stability and electrical conductivity for spin-up electrons
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum stability chemical (QSC) parameters, including dipole moment (μ), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), and chemical hardness (η) were calculated for spin-up electrons using the HOMO energy (EH) and the LUMO energy (EL). These parameters were determined using the following equations:
ρ =
E H
+
E L
2
, χ
= -
E H
+
E L
2
,
a n d
η =
E L
-
E H
2
[45,46,69]. Table 2 shows the values of these QSC parameters for the examined structures. Among them, 2SZ-3C exhibited the highest magnitude of dipole moment (μ), indicating an asymmetry in its spin-up electronic charge distribution. This suggests that 2SZ-3C experiences more active intramolecular charge transfer compared to the other structures. On the other hand, 3SZ-2C displayed the most negative chemical potential (ρ), indicating a higher tendency for spin-up electrons to escape. Additionally, the high electronegativity values for 3SZ-2C indicate its strong electron-attracting characteristics. Chemical hardness (η), which measures the resistance to charge transfer, was found to be highest in 2SZ-3C MS, indicating its greater resistance to charge transfer compared to other structures. Conversely, 3SZ-2C exhibited the lowest hardness value, suggesting lower resistance to charge transfer.

The electric conductivity of SZ and its derivatives was also computed for spin-up electrons using the equation
σ = e x p (
- E
g
2 K T
)
[70], where σ is the electric conductivity, K is Boltzmann's constant, T is the thermodynamic temperature, and Eg represents the band gap value of the configurations. A smaller Eg value at a given temperature leads to higher conductivity. It was observed that the band gaps for 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C exhibited a small variation. Consequently, 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C displayed higher electrical conductivity compared to the other studied MSs, with 2SZ-3C MS showing the lowest conductivity. The number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units and the number of SZ units in the studied MSs were found to affect the electrical conductivity as shown in Table 2. In conclusion, the calculation of QSC parameters and electrical conductivity provides valuable insights into the electronic characteristics and stability of 2SZ and its derivatives. The results suggest that SZ-based materials exhibit desirable electronic properties and could have potential applications in various electronic and optoelectronic devices.

Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) studies are crucial in revealing the electrophilic and nucleophilic sites of the designed SZ MS and its derivatives, providing valuable information for predicting reaction sites [71]. In Fig. 4 (a-e), the MEPs for SZ and its derivatives are depicted, where the negatively charged sites are represented in red, the positively charged sites in blue, and the neutral zones in green. The negative areas (red color) in the MEPs of SZ and its derivatives are clustered primarily at the left and right ends, particularly in the benzene rings. This observation suggests that these regions serve as nucleophilic sites, indicating their propensity to donate electrons during chemical reactions. Conversely, the positive zones (blue color) in the MEPs are mainly located around the terminal hydrogen atoms. These regions are indicative of electrophilic sites, implying their readiness to accept electrons in chemical reactions. Finally, the neutral regions (green color) in the MEPs correspond to areas devoid of any significant charge distribution, indicating their relative electron neutrality. Overall, the MEP analysis provides insights into the electrophilic and nucleophilic nature of different regions in SZ and its derivatives, guiding the understanding of their reactivity and potential reactivity sites. This information is valuable for predicting and understanding their chemical behavior and interactions in various applications.

In this section, we conducted a detailed study of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of various SZ MSs, including 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C. The resulting spectra are presented in Fig. 5 , and the relevant parameters are summarized in Table 3 . Our primary objective was to analyze the impact of increasing the number of SZ units and the number of carbon atoms in the bridge linking the SZ units on the UV–Vis absorption spectra. The absorption spectra are directly related to the electronic properties of the materials and are a result of electronic transitions between occupied and unoccupied molecular orbitals (MOs). Hence, the observed effects in the optical absorption peaks are expected to align with the electronic changes caused by varying the number of SZ units. Our investigation revealed that increasing the number of SZ units and carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ MSs has a significant influence on the absorption spectra of the SZ compound, resulting in a redshift, as illustrated in Fig. 5. Specifically, 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, and 3SZ-2C showed a redshift in their absorption spectra compared to the absorption spectrum of the SZ unit. The respective λmax values for these transitions are 467.67 nm, 513.42 nm, and 933.92 nm, corresponding to H → L + 5, H → L, and H → L transitions. In contrast, the absorption spectrum of the SZ MS has λmax at 309.00 nm, corresponding to the H → L + 3 transition. By comparing the absorption spectrum of SZ MS with a previous study [72], we find that the results obtained in this manuscript are in agreement with that study. The absorption spectra for SZ, 2SZ-2C, and 2SZ-3C exhibit two distinct peaks at 309.00 nm and 947.81 nm for SZ, 467.67 nm and 1412.01 nm for 2SZ-2C, and 513.42 nm and 991.5 nm for 2SZ-3C. However, for 3SZ-2C and 3SZ-3C, the longer wavelengths at 2333.73 nm and 944.32 nm appear as shoulders, while the shortest wavelengths at 933.92 nm and 584.92 nm still show a curvature shape. In conclusion, our investigation of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of various SZ MSs demonstrates the significant influence of increasing the number of SZ units and carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units on the optical properties of the SZ compound. The observed redshifts and specific transitions provide valuable insights into the electronic structure and behavior of SZ-based materials. Moreover, the agreement with previous research further validates the accuracy and reliability of our findings.

The observed current/volt curves for SZ and its based materials provide valuable insights into their electrical behavior (Fig. 6 ). The increase in electric current with increasing volt values until reaching a maximum point, followed by a subsequent decrease, is a characteristic trend often seen in electronic devices and materials. The initial increase in electric current with increasing voltage is typically associated with Ohm's law as shown in Fig. 6, which states that the electric current (I) is directly proportional to the voltage (V) across a material, provided that the material's resistance remains constant. As the voltage is increased, more charge carriers (e.g., electrons or holes) are driven through the material, resulting in a higher electric current. The maximum point observed in the current/volt curve (See Fig. 6) indicates the saturation point, where the material's ability to conduct current becomes limited. The saturation points for SZ, 2SZ-2C, 2SZ-3C, 3SZ-2C, and 3SZ-3C are at the following points (0.5, 5.68 × 10-5), (1.1, 1.05 × 10-4), (0.2, 4.85 × 10-6), (0.3, 6.35 × 10-4), and (0.3, 2.74 × 10-11) respectively. Hence, the highest saturation points are for 2SZ-2C and 3SZ-2C and the lowest saturation point is for 3SZ-3C and 2SZ-3C. Therefore, when the number of carbon atoms in the bridges between SZ units decreases the saturations, and points increase. The subsequent decrease in electric current beyond the saturation point may be attributed to several factors. One possible reason is the formation of charge carrier traps [73], which capture and immobilize charge carriers, reducing their mobility and thus decreasing the overall current flow. Additionally, at higher voltage levels, there may be increased scattering of charge carriers, leading to reduced conductivity and a drop in current intensity.

In conclusion, our comprehensive investigation into the properties of super-zethrene (SZ) and its derivatives has revealed a wealth of valuable insights across various domains. The optimized molecular structures highlighted SZ's planarity and the non-planar conformations of its derivatives, shedding light on their structural characteristics. The stability analysis, as indicated by binding energy calculations, underscores the favorable stability of SZ-based materials, with an upward trend linked to the number of SZ units and carbon atoms in the bridge between units. The examination of spin-up electronic properties uncovers consistent traits except for 2SZ-3C, and a pronounced decrease in energy gap with an increase in SZ units, particularly notable in 3SZ-2C and 2SZ-2C. Quantum stability parameters and electrical conductivity evaluations showcase these materials' electronic attributes, suggesting their potential in electronic applications. The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) studies offer insights into reactive sites, contributing to a better understanding of their chemical behavior. The UV–Vis absorption spectra showcase unique optical transitions influenced by the number of SZ units and carbon atoms, providing crucial information about their electronic structures. The observed current/voltage profiles elucidate intriguing electrical trends, highlighting saturation points that depend on the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between SZ units. In summary, our investigation contributes significantly to the understanding of SZ and its derivatives, spanning their structural, electronic, optical, and electrical properties. These findings pave the way for promising applications in electronic and optoelectronic devices, offering potential benefits across various technological domains. As future research endeavors build upon these insights, we anticipate further innovations that capitalize on the exceptional attributes of SZ and its derivatives.
Ethical approval: This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
Consent to participate: This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
Consent to publish: All authors mentioned in the manuscript have given consent for submission and subsequent publication of the manuscript.

Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Visualization, Writing – original draft. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Investigation. Hazem Abdelsalam: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – review & editing. Qinfang Zhang: Conceptualization, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Ministry of Science and Technology, Foreign Experts Program (No. QN2021014007L, QN2022014009L), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361, 20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmmm.2023.171684.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-surface light-induced generation of higher acenes and elucidation of their open-shell character

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.I. Urgel, S. Mishra, H. Hayashi, J. Wilhelm, C.A. Pignedoli, M. Di Giovannantonio, R. Widmer, M. Yamashita, N. Hieda, P. Ruffieux, On-surface light-induced generation of higher acenes and elucidation of their open-shell character, Nat. Commun. 10 (2019) 861.
J. Krüger, F. García, F. Eisenhut, D. Skidin, J.M. Alonso, E. Guitián, D. Pérez, G. Cuniberti, F. Moresco, D. Peña, Decacene: on‐surface generation, Angew. Chemie Int. Ed. 56 (2017) 11945–11948.
